plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Defection and Negotiation with the Russians
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad is received by the younger Vorontsov, then handed upward through the Russian command chain until he meets the Viceroy Mikhail Vorontsov in Tiflis to negotiate terms, offering to destroy Shamil in exchange for help rescuing his family.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Establishes the protagonist's central bargain and his precarious position between two powers

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Family Held by Shamil
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad reveals that his wife, mother, and children are in Shamil's hands and demands a prisoner exchange before he can openly serve the Russians. This becomes the central lever that constrains all his subsequent choices.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Plants the ticking-clock stakes that drive the rest of the novel

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Private Vigilance Amid Russian Hospitality
plotlines.description: Behind the courteous exterior, Hadji Murad systematically assesses threats: checks on his murids' weapons, eats only from shared dishes, keeps his dagger close, and reads the political pecking order. The silent eye-meeting with old Vorontsov crystallises the mutual mistrust.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Builds the protagonist's interiority and dramatic irony beneath diplomatic surface

plotlines.name: Peter Avdeev's Death in Hospital
plotlines.description: The wounded soldier Avdeev dies in the Vozdvizhensk hospital, dictating a final message to his family and asking for a death candle. The doctor cannot extract the bullet.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Gives a concrete, human face to the nameless 'one killed' in official reports

plotlines.name: The Avdeev Family Threshing and Mourning at Home
plotlines.description: On the day Peter dies, his family threshes oats and quarrels about him; his mother dictates a letter with a ruble which is returned with news of his death. His widow Aksinya mourns publicly but is secretly relieved because she is pregnant by a shopkeeper.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Tolstoyan counterpoint showing the domestic, economic, and moral costs of one conscript's death

plotlines.name: Aksinya's Secret Life and Pregnancy
plotlines.description: Peter's widow has left the household, works in service, is pregnant by a shopkeeper, and is inwardly glad of her husband's death while lamenting loudly in public.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Undercuts official narrative of heroic sacrifice with private moral ambiguity

plotlines.name: Jurisdictional Quarrel Between Young Vorontsov and General Meller-Zakomelsky
plotlines.description: Meller-Zakomelsky is furious that Vorontsov handled Hadji Murad's capitulation without reporting to him. The quarrel is defused by Marya Vasilevna's charm, and it is decided Hadji Murad will be routed onward.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Exposes Russian command hierarchy as driven by vanity, patronage, and family connections

plotlines.name: Viceroy Mikhail Vorontsov's Court in Tiflis
plotlines.description: The aged Viceroy receives news of Hadji Murad's surrender at a formal dinner, navigates social embarrassment over the Dargo disaster, then the next day holds audiences with petitioners and meets Hadji Murad for the first time, followed by an Italian opera and a ball.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Portrays imperial power as performance, flattery, and choreographed etiquette

plotlines.name: The Dargo Campaign Embarrassment
plotlines.description: The red-haired general inadvertently reminds the dinner table that Hadji Murad's ambush nearly wiped out Vorontsov's Dargo detachment, forcing the courtiers to euphemise a past military disaster.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Reveals how truth is socially suppressed around power; characterises Vorontsov's vanity

plotlines.name: Courtly Mythologising of Hadji Murad
plotlines.description: The dinner guests escalate Hadji Murad's reputation (Napoleon, Murat, great man, chivalrous), calibrating their praise to please Vorontsov and ignoring episodes like the ordered killing of 26 prisoners.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Shows how legend is socially manufactured; sets up ironic contrast with the real man

plotlines.name: The Official Report vs. the Lived Truth
plotlines.description: Tolstoy juxtaposes Avdeev's agonising death and family grief with the clinical sentence in the Tiflis report, and again contrasts it with the army-clerk phrase 'defending his Tsar, his Fatherland, and the Orthodox Faith.'
plotlines.dramatic_function: Core anti-imperial irony of the novel; strips rhetoric from human cost

plotlines.name: The Kunak Gift-Exchange Motif
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad gives Bulka his dagger on kunak law; the Vorontsovs reciprocate with the chiming watch. The exchange codes cultural difference and sets up later gift-transactions.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Establishes Caucasian code of hospitality vs. Russian salon world; plants motif

plotlines.name: Authorial Portrait of Viceroy Vorontsov
plotlines.description: Narrator sketches Vorontsov's English education, courtier nature, Krasnoe reputation, wealth, Crimean palace, and need for 'power and submission'.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Characterises the imperial face opposing Shamil; exposition through digression

plotlines.name: The Waiting Room of Petitioners
plotlines.description: Before Hadji Murad's audience, the narrator itemises the gallery of supplicants, each embodying a corruption or dependency of the imperial system.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Satirical tableau of empire's patronage machine; frames Hadji Murad as one more petitioner

plotlines.name: The Turban and the Klugenau-Akhmet-Khan Backstory
plotlines.description: The turban Hadji Murad wears is identified as the cause of his 1840 arrest by Klugenau after Akhmet Khan's denunciation, which drove him to Shamil. He now cites this history and his Avarian service record to Vorontsov.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Provides origin of his defection and vendetta; plants backstory for later chapters

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad Confronts Russian Culture (Opera, Ball, Half-Dressed Women)
plotlines.description: At the Italian opera and the Vorontsov ball, Hadji Murad displays Oriental indifference to bare-shouldered women, champagne, and music, answering only that 'among his people nothing of the kind was done.'
plotlines.dramatic_function: Cultural-clash set piece; sharpens the gap between his world and the Russian one

plotlines.name: Shamil as the Absent Antagonist
plotlines.description: Shamil is repeatedly invoked as the enemy whose fall is now imminent (dinner guests) and whom Hadji Murad swears to destroy, while he holds Hadji Murad's family hostage.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Keeps the off-stage antagonist pressure active; shapes Hadji Murad's motivation

plotlines.name: Marya Vasilevna as Social Lubricant
plotlines.description: Marya Vasilevna charms Hadji Murad, orchestrates the watch-gift, and single-handedly defuses the Meller-Vorontsov confrontation with a laugh and a proverb.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Shows soft power of aristocratic women; contrasts salon diplomacy with military vanity

plotlines.name: Bulka and the Dagger
plotlines.description: Six-year-old Bulka climbs onto the 'brigand's' knee, receives the dagger, and embodies innocent fascination with the warrior.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Humanises Hadji Murad; mirrors his own absent children held by Shamil

plotlines.name: Narrator on Conscription as Social Death
plotlines.description: Tolstoy pauses to explain that in those days conscription was 'like death' and a soldier was 'a severed branch', grounding the Avdeev family's fatalism.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Historical-moral framing; links private tragedy to structural violence

notes: Chapters 6 and 9-10 form a continuous Russian-reception sequence around Hadji Murad; chapters 7-8 are a self-contained Avdeev parenthesis that deliberately interrupts the main line. The Avdeev material is highly fusable for a feature (single combined mini-arc). 'Hadji Murad's family hostage' is introduced here but belongs to a much larger arc spanning later chapters.
